# Does Olympiad Math Training Improve GRE Mathematics Subject Test Performance?

**TL;DR**
- The claim that olympiad training raises GRE Math Subject Test scores is **PLAUSIBLE-BUT-UNPROVEN**: no study directly tests it, and the broad transfer literature sets a low prior probability for "far transfer" of this kind.
- The single most relevant controlled study (Rebholz, Golle, Tibus, Ruth-Herbein, Moeller & Trautwein, 2022 — a quasi-experiment with N=201 high-achieving German children) found olympiad-prep training transferred to a standardized curriculum math test at roughly 0.45 SD — but only for fourth graders, in a selected sample, without randomization.
- Most apparent "olympiad → exam" links are explained by **selection** (strong students self-select into competitions); the mechanism with the strongest *mechanism-level* empirical backing is stress/anxiety reduction, not deep schema transfer.

## Section A — The Transfer-Theory Prior

The scientific default here is skepticism. The core question — whether training on hard, novel, proof-flavored olympiad problems improves a timed, breadth-heavy, multiple-choice undergraduate exam — is a question about **far transfer**, and roughly a century of research says far transfer is rare and difficult to elicit. The GRE Mathematics Subject Test itself underscores the gap: per ETS it is ~66 five-choice questions in 2 hours 50 minutes (~2.5 minutes each), ~50% calculus, ~25% algebra, ~25% other undergraduate topics — a speeded, breadth-and-recognition exam, structurally unlike untimed creative olympiad problem-solving.

**Thorndike & Woodworth (1901): identical elements.** Edward Thorndike and Robert Woodworth's foundational experiments (e.g., training people to estimate areas) led to the "identical elements" theory: transfer occurs only to the extent that two tasks share concrete stimulus–response elements. In their words, "spread of practice occurs only where identical elements are concerned in the influencing and influenced function." This predicts that practice in a *format* (timed multiple-choice undergraduate content) transfers to that same format — not from a structurally different activity.

**Perkins & Salomon (1988/1992): low road / high road.** David Perkins and Gavriel Salomon reconciled mixed findings by positing two routes. *Low-road* transfer arises from extensive, varied practice that produces near-automatic responses triggered by stimuli similar to the training context — this drives *near* transfer. *High-road* transfer requires deliberate, mindful abstraction of a principle and a conscious search for where it applies; it is the only plausible route to *far* transfer, and it is effortful and not automatic. They note conventional instruction usually fails to establish the conditions for either route, and recommend "hugging" (making practice resemble the target) and "bridging" (explicitly teaching for abstraction).

**Barnett & Ceci (2002): a taxonomy for far transfer.** Susan Barnett and Stephen Ceci (*Psychological Bulletin*, 128, 612–637) argued that a century of debate stalled because researchers compared "apples and oranges." They proposed nine dimensions along which transfer can be near or far — a content axis (learned skill, performance change, memory demands) and a context axis (knowledge domain, physical, temporal, functional, social, and modality contexts). Olympiad → GRE differs on many of these: knowledge domain partly overlaps, but functional context (creative exploration vs. rapid item selection), modality (open construction vs. five-choice MC), and temporal/physical contexts all differ. By their framework this is squarely "far," and they conclude evidence for far transfer is real only under narrow conditions and is heavily confounded by methodology.

**Detterman (1993): "transfer is rare."** Douglas Detterman, in *Transfer on Trial* (Detterman & Sternberg, eds.), takes the strongest skeptical position: "Transfer is rare, and its likelihood of occurrence is directly related to the similarity between two situations." He argues most lab demonstrations of far transfer are artifacts of hints — "Telling subjects to use a principle is not transfer. It is following instructions." Singley & Anderson (1989) concur that transfer, when it occurs, "depends on shared or overlapping content."

**Converging negative evidence from cognitive training.** The strongest analogical evidence is the collapse of "brain-training" far-transfer claims. Simons et al. (2016, *Psychological Science in the Public Interest*) found extensive evidence that training improves the trained task, less for closely related tasks, and "little evidence" for distantly related tasks or everyday cognition. Owen, Hampshire, Grahn, Stenton, Dajani, Burns, Howard & Ballard (2010, *Nature* 465(7299):775–778) — a six-week online trial run with the BBC's *Bang Goes The Theory* that randomly assigned 11,430 participants aged 18–60 — reported that "although improvements were observed in every one of the cognitive tasks that were trained, no evidence was found for transfer effects to untrained tasks, even when those tasks were cognitively closely related." Sala & Gobet's meta-analyses of chess, music, and working-memory training repeatedly found that far-transfer effect sizes shrink toward zero as design quality improves (i.e., when active control groups are used); their second-order meta-analysis (Sala et al., 2019, *Journal of Cognition*) concluded that "when placebo effects and publication bias were controlled for, the overall effect size and true variance equaled zero... The lack of generalization of skills acquired by training is thus an invariant of human cognition." Theories of expertise (Ericsson; Simon & Chase chunking; Gobet & Simon templates) reinforce this: skill is largely domain-specific.

**Prior probability.** Combining these positions, the base rate for genuine far transfer from a structurally dissimilar activity to a different test format is **low**. Where positive findings exist, they tend to be near transfer (shared content), prompted rather than spontaneous, or confounded by selection/weak controls. The burden of proof sits firmly on anyone claiming olympiad practice *causes* GRE gains.

## Section B — Direct-Evidence Table

The honest headline: **direct evidence is thin to nearly absent.** No study tests olympiad training → GRE Math Subject Test. No study links AMC/AIME or olympiad *training* to SAT/ACT/AP gains with prior-ability controls. The closest controlled evidence is a single quasi-experiment in a much younger population.

| Study | Design | Sample / population | Outcome | Effect / direction | Confound control | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rebholz, Golle, Tibus, Ruth-Herbein, Moeller & Trautwein (2022)** — "Getting fit for the Mathematical Olympiad," *Z. Erziehungswiss.* 25(5):1175–1198 | Quasi-experimental, pre/post, non-equivalent control (not randomized) | N=201 German grade 3–4 high-achieving/gifted children; training group n=50 | Mathematical Olympiad score; standardized German DEMAT curriculum math test | Per authors: significant positive effects on competition performance "for both third and fourth graders" (B≈1.15 G3, p=.048; 0.55 G4, p=.041) and on the curriculum-oriented test "for fourth graders only" (B≈0.45, p=.010; G3 B≈0.69, p=.164, NS) | Covariate-adjusted regression: pretest math competence, fluid + crystallized IQ, math self-concept, motivation, age. Training group started ~1 SD higher (selection) | Best direct evidence; shows *some* transfer to a standardized test in one grade, but selected sample, small cells, not randomized, far younger than GRE takers |
| **Schoenfeld (1979)** — explicit heuristic instruction | Small controlled experiment, equal time, identical problems/solutions | College students | Problem-solving performance (researcher-made) | Heuristics group significantly outperformed control | Equal-time control | Shows heuristic training moves *problem-solving* outcomes, not a standardized MC exam |
| **Polya/Schoenfeld-based interventions** (e.g., Nigeria 2024; Jahudin & Siew, Malaysia; Harskamp & Suhre 2007) | Quasi-experimental / partial randomization | School/college students | Researcher-made tests (not standardized) | Positive, significant | ANCOVA on pretest | Outcomes are non-standardized researcher tests; not generalizable to the GRE format |
| **Wai, Lubinski, Benbow & Steiger (2010); Lubinski & Benbow (2006)** — SMPY | Longitudinal, correlational | 1,467+ mathematically precocious youth, 25–35 yr follow-up | STEM career / creative accomplishments (not test scores) | Enrichment "dose" associated with more STEM accomplishment | Stratifies within top 1% ability | Correlational; outcome is not test scores; both ability and dose matter |
| **General math-competition participation reviews** | Correlational / review | Mixed | Course grades, standardized scores, STEM career | Positive correlations | None / weak | Not causal; selection-confounded |

**Benchmark for what a real result looks like:** Solomon, Dupuis, O'Hara, Hockenberry, Lam, Goco, Ferguson & Tannock (2019, *PLOS ONE* 14(10):e0223049; trial NCT02456181) — a two-year Ontario cluster-RCT of 554 grade-2 and 592 grade-5 students (N=1,146) and 193 teachers across 41 schools, randomly assigned to JUMP Math vs. business-as-usual problem-based instruction — found significant computation gains on standardized + curriculum measures but *no* significant difference in problem solving. Even a strong RCT yields narrow, content-specific gains, not broad problem-solving transfer.

**Verdict on the evidence base: THIN.** One quasi-experiment in 9-year-olds is the entire direct-causal foundation, and it does not use the GRE format or population.

## Section C — Mechanisms Table

Only if some benefit is plausible do mechanisms matter. For each proposed channel I label evidence as **[empirical]** (direct experimental evidence it moves a relevant score) or **[theoretical/plausible]** (a coherent story but no direct exam-score evidence), then ask whether it plausibly moves a *timed multiple-choice* score.

| Channel | Empirical or theoretical | Evidence strength | Does it plausibly move a timed-MC score? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fluency / automaticity of algebraic & computational moves** | [empirical] for near transfer; [theoretical] for olympiad→GRE | Moderate. Perkins & Salomon's low road (automaticity via varied practice) is well-grounded; but olympiad problems emphasize novelty over the speeded routine computation the GRE rewards | **Yes, if the practice rehearses GRE-type moves** — but olympiad practice is not optimized for fast routine calculus/algebra. The identical-elements view favors GRE-format drilling here |
| **Comfort with novelty / reduced freeze-up on unfamiliar items** | [theoretical/plausible] | Weak-to-moderate; no direct exam-score evidence; consistent with exposure/expertise logic | Plausibly yes — the GRE rewards not panicking on odd items — but unmeasured for this transfer |
| **Test anxiety / stress inoculation** | [empirical] (for the mechanism generally) | Moderate-to-strong for the general effect: Saunders, Driskell, Johnston & Salas (1996, *J. Occupational Health Psychology* 1(2):170–186), "based on a total of 37 studies with 70 separate hypothesis tests, representing the behavior of 1,837 participants," concluded stress-inoculation training is "an effective means for reducing state anxiety, reducing skill-specific anxiety, and enhancing performance under stress." Competition exposure is not formal SIT, so application is inferential | Plausibly yes — the channel with the strongest *mechanism-level* empirical backing, and timed high-pressure exams are exactly where anxiety reduction matters. But evidence it operates *via competition exposure* is indirect |
| **Faster pattern recognition / heuristic repertoire (Polya, Schoenfeld)** | [empirical] for problem-solving tasks; [theoretical] for MC exam transfer | Moderate for problem-solving outcomes (Schoenfeld 1979); but Polya's heuristics are criticized as too general to reliably transfer, and Detterman warns prompted use isn't spontaneous transfer | Mixed — heuristics (work backward, try small cases, find counterexamples, eliminate answers) map *well* onto GRE MC tactics, so this is the most promising content channel, but unproven on standardized scores |
| **Deeper conceptual schemas making standard problems trivial** | [empirical] (schema theory) within domain; [theoretical] for far transfer | Moderate within domain (Sweller's cognitive-load and worked-example research shows schemas distinguish experts; worked examples beat unguided problem-solving for *acquisition*). Schemas are domain-specific, so transfer is bounded | Yes *where the GRE tests the same concepts olympiad work deepened* (e.g., number theory, combinatorics, algebra) — i.e., this is really near transfer dressed up as far transfer |

**Reading the table:** The channels split into (1) a genuinely empirically-backed but *indirect* one (anxiety/stress inoculation), and (2) content channels (schemas, heuristics, fluency) that only "work" to the extent olympiad practice happens to overlap GRE content — precisely the near-transfer caveat from Thorndike, Barnett & Ceci, and Detterman. None has been shown to move a timed MC undergraduate math score via olympiad training specifically.

## Section D — Causal-Claim Verdict

**Verdict: PLAUSIBLE-BUT-UNPROVEN, leaning toward "helps strong students who'd score well anyway" rather than "causes gains for a typical taker."**

The blunt skeptic's case:
1. **Selection dominates.** Strong students self-select into olympiad training. Raw correlations between competition experience and exam scores are not causal. The one controlled study that adjusted for baseline (Rebholz/Trautwein 2022) found the training group started ~1 SD ahead — exactly the selection effect skeptics predict — and even after adjustment the standardized-test transfer was significant in only one of two grades.
2. **The transfer is "far" by Barnett & Ceci's axes**, and the prior probability is low (Detterman; Sala & Gobet; Simons et al.; Owen et al.).
3. **Opportunity cost.** Time on olympiad problems is time not spent on GRE-format practice. The identical-elements view predicts format-specific practice (timed five-choice, undergraduate-content drills under ~2.5 min/item) wins for raising *this* score. Olympiad problems are largely untimed, proof-oriented, and reward depth over the breadth-and-speed the GRE rewards.
4. **Publication bias and the lab-vs-exam gap.** Positive heuristic/problem-solving studies typically use researcher-made tests, small samples, and weak controls; the brain-training saga shows how far-transfer claims evaporate under active-control designs. Discount rosy practitioner claims (e.g., test-prep marketing that competition "eliminates test anxiety").

Where a real benefit is most defensible: a strong student whose olympiad work *overlaps* GRE content (number theory, combinatorics, algebra) and who has been *inoculated against timed-test stress* may convert that into marginal GRE gains — but this is near transfer plus anxiety reduction, not a far-transfer dividend, and it likely helps the already-strong more than the median taker.

**The study that would settle it:** A randomized controlled trial that takes students **matched on baseline ability** (e.g., a diagnostic GRE Math pretest plus prior math GPA), randomly assigns them to either (a) olympiad-style problem-solving training or (b) GRE-format timed multiple-choice practice of equal time and intensity, with a passive control, and measures the **actual GRE Math Subject Test** (or a validated parallel form) as the outcome. Active control + equal time isolates the active ingredient; baseline matching removes the selection confound; the real exam as outcome closes the lab-vs-exam gap. The decisive prediction from transfer theory: **format-matched practice should win**, with olympiad training showing benefit only on the content-overlap and anxiety margins.

## Self-Confidence Summary
- Confidence in the transfer-theory prior (far transfer is rare): **High** — multiple converging meta-analyses and seminal sources.
- Confidence that direct olympiad→GRE evidence is essentially absent: **High** — exhaustive search found none.
- Confidence in the single positive transfer datapoint (Rebholz/Trautwein 2022): **Moderate** — peer-reviewed and open-access, but quasi-experimental, young sample, mixed results.
- Confidence in mechanism ratings: **Moderate**.

**One-line verdict:** The claim "olympiad practice raises GRE Subject performance" is **not empirically supported** as a causal statement; it is plausible only through near-transfer content overlap and anxiety reduction, and the **single biggest evidence gap is the complete absence of any ability-controlled study using olympiad training and a standardized, timed multiple-choice math exam as the outcome.**

## Sources

**Peer-reviewed**
- Barnett, S. M., & Ceci, S. J. (2002). When and where do we apply what we learn? A taxonomy for far transfer. *Psychological Bulletin*, 128(4), 612–637. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-2909.128.4.612
- Thorndike, E. L., & Woodworth, R. S. (1901). The influence of improvement in one mental function upon the efficiency of other functions. *Psychological Review*. https://psychclassics.yorku.ca/Thorndike/Transfer/transfer1.htm
- Salomon, G., & Perkins, D. N. (1989). Rocky roads to transfer: Rethinking mechanisms of a neglected phenomenon. *Educational Psychologist*, 24(2), 113–142. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15326985ep2402_1 (see also Perkins & Salomon, 1992, "Transfer of Learning," *International Encyclopedia of Education*, 2nd ed.)
- Simons, D. J., Boot, W. R., Charness, N., Gathercole, S. E., Chabris, C. F., Hambrick, D. Z., & Stine-Morrow, E. A. L. (2016). Do "Brain-Training" Programs Work? *Psychological Science in the Public Interest*, 17(3), 103–186. https://doi.org/10.1177/1529100616661983
- Owen, A. M., Hampshire, A., Grahn, J. A., Stenton, R., Dajani, S., Burns, A. S., Howard, R. J., & Ballard, C. G. (2010). Putting brain training to the test. *Nature*, 465(7299), 775–778. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature09042
- Sala, G., & Gobet, F. (2017). Does far transfer exist? Negative evidence from chess, music, and working memory training. *Current Directions in Psychological Science*, 26(6), 515–520. https://doi.org/10.1177/0963721417712760
- Sala, G., et al. (2019). Near and far transfer in cognitive training: A second-order meta-analysis. *Journal of Cognition* / *Collabra: Psychology*. https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.203
- Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning. *Cognitive Science*, 12(2), 257–285. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15516709cog1202_4

- Solomon, T., Dupuis, A., O'Hara, A., Hockenberry, M.-N., Lam, J., Goco, G., Ferguson, B., & Tannock, R. (2019). A cluster randomized-controlled trial of the impact of the JUMP Math program. *PLOS ONE*, 14(10), e0223049. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0223049
- Wai, J., Lubinski, D., Benbow, C. P., & Steiger, J. H. (2010). Accomplishment in STEM and its relation to STEM educational dose: A 25-year longitudinal study. *Journal of Educational Psychology*, 102(4), 860–871.
- Lubinski, D., & Benbow, C. P. (2006). Study of Mathematically Precocious Youth after 35 years. *Perspectives on Psychological Science*, 1(4), 316–345. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1745-6916.2006.00019.x

**Institutional / book**
- Detterman, D. K., & Sternberg, R. J. (eds.) (1993). *Transfer on Trial: Intelligence, Cognition, and Instruction.* Ablex/Praeger.
- Schoenfeld, A. H. (1985). *Mathematical Problem Solving.* Academic Press.
- Polya, G. (1945). *How to Solve It.* Princeton University Press.

- AwesomeMath (affiliated with Titu Andreescu), "Why Participate in Math Competitions? 10 Powerful Benefits." (Practitioner claims that competition "eliminates test anxiety" and that participants complete SAT/ACT math "faster and more accurately"; treated here as uncontrolled marketing, not evidence.) https://www.awesomemath.org/why-participate-in-math-competitions/
